title: Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Embeded Domain-Specific Language [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Embeded Domain-Specific Language

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## Abstract

Reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) suffers from the issue of having too much space overhead to trace back intermediate computational states for back-propagation. The traditional method to trace back states is called checkpointing that stores intermediate states into a global stack and restore state through either stack pop or re-computing. The overhead of stack manipulations and re-computing makes the general purposed (not tensor-based) AD engines unable to meet many industrial needs. Instead of checkpointing, we propose to use reverse computing to trace back states by designing and implementing a reversible programming eDSL, where a program can be executed bi-directionally without implicit stack operations. The absence of implicit stack operations makes the program compatible with existing compiler features, including utilizing existing optimization passes and compiling the code as GPU kernels. We implement AD for sparse matrix operations and some machine learning applications to show that our framework has the state-of-the-art performance.

## 1 Introduction

Most of the popular automatic differentiation (AD) tools in the market, such as TensorFlow (Abadi et al., 2015), Pytorch (Paszke et al., 2017), and Flux (Innes et al., 2018) implements reverse mode AD at the tensor level to meet the need in machine learning. Later, People in the scientific computing domain also realized the power of these AD tools, they use these tools to solve scientific problems such as seismic inversion (Zhu et al., 2020), variational quantum circuits simulation (Bergholm et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019) and variational tensor network simulation (Liao et al., 2019; Roberts et al., 2019). To meet the diverse need in these applications, one sometimes has to define backward rules manually, for example

1. To differentiate sparse matrix operations used in Hamiltonian engineering (Hao Xie & Wang), people defined backward rules for sparse matrix multiplication and dominant eigensolvers (Golub & Van Loan, 2012),

2. In tensor network algorithms to study the phase transition problem (Liao et al., 2019; Seeger et al., 2017; Wan & Zhang, 2019; Hubig, 2019), people defined backward rules for singular value decomposition (SVD) function and QR decomposition (Golub & Van Loan, 2012).

Instead of defining backward rules manually, one can also use a general purposed AD (GP-AD) framework like Tapenade (Hascoet & Pascual, 2013), OpenAD (Utke et al., 2008) and Zygote (Innes, 2018; Innes et al., 2019). Researchers have used these tools in practical applications such as bundle adjustment (Shen & Dai, 2018) and earth system simulation (Forget et al., 2015), where differentiating scalar operations is important. However, the power of these tools are often limited by their relatively poor performance. In many practical applications, a program might do billions of computations. In each computational step, the AD engine might cache some data for backpropagation. (Griewank & Walther, 2008) Frequent caching of data slows down the program significantly, while the memory usage will become a bottleneck as well. Caching implicitly also make these frameworks incompatible with kernel functions. To avoid such issues, we need a new GP-AD framework that does not cache automatically for users.
In this paper, we propose to implement the reverse mode AD on a reversible (domain-specific) programming language (Perumalla, 2013; Frank, 2017), where intermediate states can be traced

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

backward without accessing an implicit stack. Reversible programming allows people to utilize the reversibility to reverse a program. In machine learning, reversibility is proven to substantially decrease the memory usage in unitary recurrent neural networks (MacKay et al., 2018), normalizing flow (Dinh et al., 2014), hyper-parameter learning (Maclaurin et al., 2015) and residual neural networks (Gomez et al., 2017; Behrmann et al., 2018). Reversible programming will make these happen naturally. The power of reversible programming is not limited to handling these reversible applications, any program can be written in a reversible style. Converting an irreversible program to the reversible form would cost overheads in time and space. Reversible programming provides a flexible time-space trade-off scheme that different with checkpointing (Griewank, 1992; Griewank & Walther, 2008; Chen et al., 2016), _reverse computing_ (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990), to let user handle these overheads explicitly. There have been many prototypes of reversible languages like Janus (Lutz, 1986), R (not the popular one) (Frank, 1997), Erlang (Lanese et al., 2018) and object-oriented ROOPL (Haulund, 2017). In the past, the primary motivation to study reversible programming is to support reversible computing devices (Frank & Knight Jr, 1999) such as adiabatic complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) (Koller & Athas, 1992), molecular mechanical computing system (Merkle et al., 2018) and superconducting system (Likharev, 1977; Semenov et al., 2003; Takeuchi et al., 2014; 2017), and these reversible computing devices are orders more energy-efficient. Landauer proves that only when a device does not erase information (i.e. reversible), its energy efficiency can go beyond the thermal dynamic limit. (Landauer, 1961; Reeb & Wolf, 2014) However, these reversible programming languages can not be used directly in real scientific computing, since most of them do not have basic elements like floating point numbers, arrays, and complex numbers. This motivates us to build a new embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) in Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017) as a new playground of GP-AD.

In this paper, we first compare the time-space trade-off in the optimal checkpointing and the optimal reverse computing in Sec. 2. Then we introduce the language design of NiLang in Sec. 3. In Sec. 4, we explain the implementation of automatic differentiation in NiLang. In Sec. 5, we benchmark the performance of NiLang’s AD with other AD software and explain why it is fast.

## 2 Reverse computing as an Alternative to Checkpointing

One can use either checkpointing or reverse computing to trace back intermediate states of a _T_ - step computational process _s_ 1 = _f_ 1( _s_ 0), _s_ 2 = _f_ 2( _s_ 1), . . . , _sT_ = _fT_ ( _sT_ −1) with a run-time memory _S_ . In the checkpointing scheme, the program first takes snapshots of states at certain time steps _S_ = { _sa_ , _sb_ , . . .}, 1 ≤ _a_ < _b_ < ... ≤ _T_ by running a forward pass. When retrieving a state _sk_ , if _sk_ ∈ _S_ , just return this state, otherwise, return max _s j_ < _k_ ∈ _S_ and re-compute _sk_ from _s j_ . In the reverse _j_ computing scheme, one first writes the program in a reversible style. Without prior knowledge, a regular program can be transpiled to the reversible style is by doing the transformation in Listing. 1.

Listing 1: Transpiling a regular code to the reversible code without prior knowledge.

Listing 2: The reverse of Listing. 1

Then one can visit states in the reversed order by running the reversed program in Listing. 2, which erases the computed results from the tail. One may argue that easing through uncomputing is not necessary here. This is not true for a general reversible program, because the intermediate states might be mutable and used in other parts of the program. It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step. On the other side, only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead by recomputing everything from the beginning _s_ 0, with a time complexity _O_ ( _T_[2] ). The minimum space complexity in reverse

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

computing is _O_ ( _S_ log( _T_ / _S_ )) (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990; Perumalla, 2013), with time complexity O( _T_[1][.][585] ).

||**Method**|most time efcient
(Time/Space)|most space efcient
(Time/Space)|
|---|---|---|---|
|Checkpointing||O(_T_)/O(_T_ +_S_)|O(_T_ 2)/O(_S_)|
|Reverse computing||O(_T_)/O(_T_ +_S_)|O(_T_( _T_
_S_ )0.585)/O(_S_ log( _T_
_S_ ))|
Table 1: _T_ and _S_ are the time and space of the original irreversible program. In the “Reverse computing” case, the reversibility of the original program is not utilized.

The difference in space overheads can be explained by the difference of the optimal checkpointing and optimal reverse computing algorithms. The optimal checkpointing algorithm that widely used in AD is the treeverse algorithm in Fig. 1(a). This algorithm partitions the computational process **binomially** into _d_ sectors. At the beginning of each sector, the program snapshots the state and push it into a global stack, hence the memory for checkpointing is _dS_ . The states in the last sector are retrieved by the above space-efficient _O_ ( _T_[2] ) algorithm. After that, the last snapshot can be freed and the program has one more quota in memory. With the freed memory, the second last sector can be further partition into two sectors. Likewise, the _l_ th sectors is partitioned into _l_ sub-sectors, where _l_ is the sector index counting from the tail. Recursively apply this treeverse algorithm _t_ times until the sector size is 1. The approximated overhead in time and space are
where _T_ = η( _t_ , _d_ ) holds. By carefully choosing either a _t_ or _d_ , the overhead in time and space can be both logarithmic.

On the other side, the optimal time-space trade-off scheme in reverse computing is the Bennett’s algorithm illustrated in Fig. 1 (b). It evenly **evenly** partition the program into _k_ sectors. The program marches forward ( _P_ process) for _k_ steps to obtain the final state _sk_ +1, then backward ( _Q_ process) from the _k_ − 1th step to erase the states in between _s_ 1< _i_ < _k_ . This process is also called the _compute-copyuncompute_ process. Recursively apply the compute-copy-uncompute process for each sector until each _P_ / _Q_ process contains only one unit computation. the time and space complexities are

Here, the overhead in time is polynomial, which is worse than the treeverse algorithm. The treeverse like partition does not apply here because the first sweep to create initial checkpoints without introducing any space overheads is not possible in reversible computing. The pseudo-code of Bennett’s time-space trade-off algorithm is shown in Listing. 3.

Listing 3: The Bennett’s time-space trade-off scheme. The first argument `{` _s_ 1 `,...}` is the collection of states, `k` is the number of partitions, `i` and `len` are the starting point and length of the working sector. A function call changes variables inplace. “∼” is the symbol of uncomputing, which means undoing a function call. Statement _si_ +1 ← 0 allocates a zero state and add it to the state collection. Its inverse _si_ +1 → 0 discards a zero cleared state from the collection. Its NiLang implementation is in Appendix A.

`bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i, len) if len == 1` _si_ +1 ← `0` _fi_ `(` _si_ +1 `,` _si_ `) else # P process that calls the forward program k steps bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i+len` ÷ `k*(j-1), len` ÷ `k) for j=1,2,...,k # Q process that calls the backward program k-1 steps ~bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i+len` ÷ `k*(j-1), len` ÷ `k) for j=k-1,k-2,...,1`

The reverse computing does not show advantage from the above complexity analysis. But we argue the this analysis is from the worst case, which are very different to the practical using cases. _First_ ,

> (ττ+!δδ!)! is the binomial function. (b) Bennett’s time space trade-off scheme for reverse computing. (Bennett, 1973; Levine & Sherman, 1990) _P_ and _Q_ are computing and uncomputing respectively. The pseudo-code is defined in Listing. 3.

reverse computing can make use of the reversibility to save memory. In Appendix B.2, we show how to implement a unitary matrix multiplication without introducing overheads in space and time. _Second_ , reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users, user can optimize the memory access patterns for their own devices like GPU. _Third_ , reverse computing is compatible with effective codes, so that it fits better with modern languages. In Appendix B.1, we show how to manipulate inplace functions on arrays with NiLang. _Fourth_ , reverse computing can utilize the existing compiler to optimize the code because it does not introduce global stack operations that harm the purity of functions. _Fifth_ , reverse computing encourages users to think reversibly. In Appendix B.3, we show reversible thinking can lead the user to a constant memory, constant time implementation of chained multiplication algorithms.

## 3 Language design

NiLang is an embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) NiLang built on top of the host language Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017). Julia is a popular language for scientific programming and machine learning. We choose Julia mainly for speed. Julia is a language with high abstraction, however, its clever design of type inference and just in time compiling make it has a C like speed. Meanwhile, it has rich features for meta-programming. Its package for pattern matching MLStyle allows us to define an eDSL in less than 2000 lines. Comparing with a regular reversible programming language, NiLang features array operations, rich number systems including floating-point numbers, complex numbers, fixed-point numbers, and logarithmic numbers. It also implements the compute-copy-uncompute (Bennett, 1973) macro to increase code reusability. Besides the above reversible hardware compatible features, it also has some reversible hardware incompatible features to meet the practical needs. For example, it views the floating-point + and − operations as reversible. It also allows users to extend instruction sets and sometimes inserting external statements. These features are not compatible with future reversible hardware. NiLang’s compiling process, grammar and operational semantics are described in Appendix G. The source code is also available online, we will put a link here after the anonymous open review session. By the time of writing, the version of NiLang is v0.7.3.

## 3.1 Reversible functions and instructions

Mathematically, any irreversible mapping `y = f(args...)` can be trivially transformed to its reversible form `y += f(args...)` or `y` ⊻ `= f(args...)` (⊻ is the bit-wise `XOR` ), where `y` is a pre-emptied variable. But in numeric computing with finite precision, this is not always true. The reversibility of arithmetic instruction is closely related to the number system. For integer and fixed

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

point number system, `y += f(args...)` and `y -= f(args...)` are rigorously reversible. For logarithmic number system and tropical number system (Speyer & Sturmfels, 2009), `y *= f(args...)` and `y /= f(args...)` as reversible (not introducing the zero element). While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation. Other reversible operations includes `SWAP` , `ROT` , `NEG` et. al., and this instruction set is extensible. One can define a reversible multiplier in NiLang as in Listing. 4.

Listing 4: A reversible multiplier

```
julia>usingNiLang
julia>@ifunctionmultiplier(y!::Real,a::Real,b::Real)
y!+=a*b
end
julia>multiplier(2,3,5)
(17,3,5)
julia>(~multiplier)(17,3,5)
(2,3,5)
```

Macro `@i` generates two functions that are reversible to each other, `multiplier` and ∼ `multiplier` , each defines a mapping R[3] → R[3] . The ! after a symbol is a part of the name, as a conversion to indicate the mutated variables.

## 3.2 Reversible memory management

A distinct feature of reversible memory management is that the content of a variable must be known when it is deallocated. We denote the allocation of a pre-emptied memory as `x` ← `0` , and its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0` . An unknown variable can not be deallocate, but can be pushed to a stack pop out later in the uncomputing stage. If a variable is allocated and deallocated in the local scope, we call it an ancilla. Listing. 5 defines the complex valued accumulative log function.
Listing 5: Reversible complex valued log Listing 6: Compute-copy-uncompute verfunction _y_ += log(| _x_ |) + _i_ Arg( _x_ ). sion of Listing. 5

|`@i @inline function (:+=)(log)(y!::Complex{T`
`}, x::Complex{T}) where T`
`n` ←`zero(T)`
`n += abs(x)`
`y!.re += log(n)`
`y!.im += angle(x)`
`n -= abs(x)`
`n` →`zero(T)`
`end`|`@i @inline function (:+=)(log)(y!::Complex{T`
`}, x::Complex{T}) where T`
`@routine begin`
`n` ←`zero(T)`
`n += abs(x)`|
|---|---|
||`end`
`y!.re += log(n)`
`y!.im += angle(x)`
`~@routine`
`end`|

Here, the macro `@inline` tells the compiler that this function can be inlined. One can input “←” and “→” by typing “\leftarrow[TAB KEY]” and “\rightarrow[TAB KEY]” respectively in a Julia editor or REPL. NiLang does not have immutable structs, so that the real part `y!.re` and imaginary `y!.im` of a complex number can be changed directly. It is easy to verify that the bottom two lines in the function body are the inverse of the top two lines. i.e., the bottom two lines _uncomputes_ the top two lines. The motivation of uncomputing is to zero clear the contents in ancilla `n` so that it can be deallocated correctly. _Compute-copy-uncompute_ is a useful design pattern in reversible programming so that we created a pair of macros `@routine` and ∼ `@routine` for it. One can rewrite the above function as in Listing. 6.

Figure 2: The flow chart for reversible (a) `if` statement and (b) `while` statement. “pre” and “post” represents precondition and postcondition respectively. The assersion errors are thrown to the host language instead of handling them in NiLang.

- 3.3 Reversible control flows

One can define reversible `if` , `for` and `while` statements in a reversible program. Fig. 2 (a) shows the flow chart of executing the reversible `if` statement. There are two condition expressions in this chart, a precondition and a postcondition. The precondition decides which branch to enter in the forward execution, while the postcondition decides which branch to enter in the backward execution. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 7 and Listing. 8.

Listing 7: Translating a reversible `if` statement (forward)

Listing 8: Translating a reversible `if` statement (backward)

|`branchkeeper = precondition`
`if precondition`
`branch A`
`else`
`branch B`
`end`
`assert branchkeeper ==`
`postcondition`|`branchkeeper = postcondition`
`if postcondition`
`~(branch A)`
`else`
`~(branch B)`
`end`
`assert branchkeeper ==`
`precondition`|
|---|---|

Fig. 2 (b) shows the flow chart of the reversible `while` statement. It also has two condition expressions. Before executing the condition expressions, the program presumes the postcondition is false. After each iteration, the program asserts the postcondition to be true. To reverse this statement, one can exchange the precondition and postcondition, and reverse the body statements. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 9 and Listing. 10.

Listing 9: Translating a reversible `while` statement (forward)

Listing 10: Translating a reversible `while` statement (backward)

|`assert postcondition == false`
`while precondition`
`loop body`
`assert postcondition == true`
`end`|`assert precondition == false`
`while postcondition`
`~(loop body)`
`assert precondition == true`
`end`|
|---|---|

The reversible `for` statement is similar to the irreversible one except that after execution, the program will assert the iterator to be unchanged. To reverse this statement, one can exchange `start` and `stop` and inverse the sign of `step` . Listing. 11 computes the Fibonacci number recursively and reversibly.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Listing 11: Computing Fibonacci number recursively and reversibly.

`@i function rrfib(out!, n) @invcheckoff if (n >= 1, ~) counter` ← `0 counter += n while (counter > 1, counter!=n) rrfib(out!, counter-1) counter -= 2 end counter -= n % 2 counter` → `0 end out! += 1 end`

Here, `out!` is an integer initialized to `0` for storing outputs. The precondition and postcondition are wrapped into a tuple. In the `if` statement, the postcondition is the same as the precondition, hence we omit the postcondition by inserting a "∼" in the second field for “copying the precondition in this field as the postcondition”. In the while statement, the postcondition is true only for the initial loop. Once code is proven correct, one can turn off the reversibility check by adding `@invcheckoff` before a statement. This will remove the reversibility check and make the code faster and compatible with GPU kernels (kernel functions can not handle exceptions).

## 4 Reversible automatic differentiation

- 4.1 Back propagation

**----- Start of picture text -----**

original program O gradient program for O
reverse reverse
reverse program R gradient program for R
compute
**----- End of picture text -----**

We decompose the problem of reverse mode AD into two sub-problems, **reversing the code** and **computing** ∂[multiple outputs]∂[single input][.][Reversing the code is trivial in reversible programming.][Computing the] gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation that computes[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]][.] Inspired by the Julia package ForwardDiff (Revels et al., 2016), we use the operator overloading technique to differentiate the program efficiently. In the backward pass, we wrap each output variable with a composite type `GVar` that containing an extra gradient field, and feed it into the reversed generic program. Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of `GVar` at the meantime of uncomputing. By reversing this gradient program, we can obtain the gradient program for the reversed program too. One can define the adjoint (“adjoint” here means the program for back-propagating gradients) of a primitive instruction as a reversible function on **either** the function itself or its reverse since the adjoint of a function’s reverse is equivalent to the reverse of the function’s adjoint.
It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_[=][ 1][.]

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

The implementation details are described in Appendix C. In most languages, operator overloading is accompanied with significant overheads of function calls and object allocation and deallocation. But in a language with type inference and just in time compiling like Julia, the boundary between two approaches are vague. The compiler inlines small functions, packs an array of constant sized immutable objects into a continuous memory, and truncates unnecessary branches automatically.

## 4.2 Hessians

Combining forward mode AD and reverse mode AD is a simple yet efficient way to obtain Hessians. By wrapping the elementary type with `Dual` defined in package ForwardDiff and throwing it into the gradient program defined in NiLang, one obtains one row/column of the Hessian matrix. We will use this approach to compute Hessians in the graph embedding benchmark in Sec. D.2.

## 4.3 CUDA kernels

CUDA programming is playing a significant role in high-performance computing. In Julia, one can write GPU compatible functions in native Julia language with KernelAbstractions. (Besard et al., 2017) Since NiLang does not push variables into stack automatically for users, it is safe to write differentiable GPU kernels with NiLang. We will differentiate CUDA kernels with no more than extra 10 lines in the bundle adjustment benchmark in Sec. 5.

## 5 Benchmarks

We benchmark our framework with the state-of-the-art GP-AD frameworks, including source code transformation based Tapenade and Zygote and operator overloading based ForwardDiff and ReverseDiff. Since most tensor based AD software like famous TensorFlow and PyTorch are not designed for the using cases used in our benchmarks, we do not include those package to avoid an unfair comparison. In the following benchmarks, the CPU device is Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6230 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and the GPU device is NVIDIA Titan V. For NiLang benchmarks, we have turned the reversibility check off to achieve a better performance.

We reproduced the benchmarks for Gaussian mixture model (GMM) and bundle adjustment in Srajer et al. (2018) by re-writing the programs in a reversible style. We show the results in Fig. 3. The Tapenade data is obtained by executing the docker file provided by the original benchmark, which provides a baseline for comparison.

model is the matrix vector multiplication, traditional AD can already handle this function well. The extra memory used to reverse the program is negligible comparing to the original program as shown in Fig. 4. The backward pass is not shown here, it is just two times the reversible program in order to store gradients. The data is obtained by counting the main memory allocations in the program manually. The analytical expression of memory usage in unit of floating point number is

where _d_ and _k_ are the size and number of covariance matrices. _P_ = _[d]_[(] _[d]_[+][1][)] _k_ + _k_ + _dk_ is the size of 2 parameter space. The memory of the dataset ( _d_ × _N_ ) is not included because it will scale as _N_ . Due to the hardness of estimating peak memory usage, the Tapenade data is missing here. The ForwardDiff memory usage is approximately the original size times the batch size, where the batch size is 12 by default.

In the bundle adjustment benchmark, NiLang performs the best on CPU. We also compiled our adjoint program to GPU with no more than 10 lines of code with KernelAbstractions, which provides another ∼200× speed up. Parallelizing the adjoint code requires the forward code not reading the same variable simultaneously in different threads, and this requirement is satisfied here. The peak memory of the original program and the reversible program are both equal to the size of parameter space because all “allocation”s happen on registers in this application.
One can find more benchmarks in Appendix D, including differentiating sparse matrix dot product and obtaining Hessians in the graph embedding application.

## 6 Discussion

In this work, we demonstrate a new approach to back propagates a program called reverse computing AD by designing a reversible eDSL NiLang. NiLang is a powerful tool to differentiate code from the source code level so that can be directly useful to machine learning researches. It can generate efficient backward rules, which is exemplified in Appendix E. It can also be used to differentiate reversible neural networks like normalizing flows (Kobyzev et al., 2019) to save memory, e.g. backpropagating NICE network (Dinh et al., 2014) with only constant space overheads. NiLang is most useful in solving large scale scientific problems memory efficiently. In Liu et al. (2020), people solve the ground state problem of a 28 × 28 square lattice spin-glass by re-writing the quantum simulator with NiLang. There are some challenges in reverse computing AD too.
- The native BLAS and convolution operations in NiLang are not optimized for the memory layout, and are too slow comparing with state of the art machine learning libraries. We

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- need a better implementation of these functions in the reversible programming context so that it can be more useful in training traditional deep neural networks.
- Although we show some examples of training neural networks on GPU, the shared-reading of a variable is not allowed.

- NiLang’s IR does not have variable analysis. The uncomputing pass is not always necessary for the irreversible host language to deallocate memory. In many cases, the host language’s variable analysis can figure this out, but it is not guarantted.

Another interesting issue is how to make use of reversible computing devices to save energy in machine learning. Reversible computing is not always more energy efficient than irreversible computing. In the time-space trade-off scheme in Sec. 2, we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to _n_ as _Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ , and the computing energy also increases exponentially. On the other side, the amount of energy to erase a unit of memory is a constant. When (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ > 1/ξ, erasing the memory irreversibly is more energy-efficient, where ξ is the energy ratio between a reversible operation (an instruction or a gate) and its irreversible counterpart.

## Acknowledgments

The authors are grateful to the people who help improve this work and fundings that sponsored the research. To meet the anonymous criteria, we will add the acknowledgments after the open review session.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an innovative approach to reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) by utilizing a reversible embedded Domain-Specific Language (eDSL) named NiLang in Julia. It moves away from traditional checkpointing toward 'reverse computing' to mitigate memory overhead. While the conceptual framing of time-space tradeoffs (comparing Bennett’s algorithm and binomial checkpointing) is insightful and the performance results on GMM and Bundle Adjustment are impressive, there are several localized technical inconsistencies in the formal presentation of algorithms and some ambiguity regarding the implementation of arithmetic reversibility on floating-point hardware.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Bennett's Algorithm time complexity
comments.quote: The minimum space complexity in reverse computing is $O(S \log(T/S))$ (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990; Perumalla, 2013), with time complexity O($T^{1.585}$).
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors state the time complexity is $O(T^{1.585})$. However, Table 1 lists the space-efficient reverse computing time as $O(T(T/S)^{0.585})$. While these are related (the exponent 0.585 comes from $\log_2 3 - 1$), the text implies a fixed exponent whereas the table shows it depends on the space $S$. More importantly, standard results for Bennett's algorithm (1989) show that for a pebble game with $k$ pebbles, the time is $O(T^{\log_2(k+1)})$ or similar polynomial forms. Initially, I thought this might be a typo, but since both $1.585$ and $0.585$ appear in different places without explicitly defining the branching factor $k$ used to derive them, it remains ambiguous.

comments.title: Subscript/Index Error in Binomial Overhead Formula
comments.quote:
The approximated overhead in time and space are
$$\text{time}: \frac{\tau}{T} \approx \frac{t}{d!^{1/t}}, \quad \text{space}: \frac{\delta}{S} \approx d$$
comments.explanation: The formula provided for time overhead $\frac{\tau}{T} \approx \frac{t}{d!^{1/t}}$ appears to contain a derivation error or a missing factor. According to Griewank (1992) and the 'treeverse' properties mentioned, the time overhead for optimal checkpointing is logarithmic ($O(\log T)$). The expression $\frac{t}{d!^{1/t}}$ is not standard for describing binomial checkpointing overhead (usually expressed via the repetition factor). Furthermore, the variable $d!$ looks suspicious in a denominator of a power. I checked if this was an OCR error, but it does not resolve to the standard $\binom{t+d}{d}$ related bounds usually seen in AD literature.

comments.title: Logic Inconsistency in Reversible 'while' Semantics
comments.quote: Fig. 2 (b) shows the flow chart of the reversible while statement... Before executing the condition expressions, the program presumes the postcondition is false. After each iteration, the program asserts the postcondition to be true.
comments.explanation: In reversible logic (like Janus), a 'while' loop requires that the precondition is true only when entering/continuing the loop and the postcondition is true only when exiting. However, the text says 'After each iteration, the program asserts the postcondition to be true.' If the postcondition is true after the first iteration, and the postcondition is the exit condition (as per the flowchart), the loop would terminate immediately or fail an assertion. Listing 9 says 'assert postcondition == true' inside the loop body before the 'end'. This would mean the loop can only ever run for iterations where the postcondition holds, which contradicts the standard Janus-style 'loop-until' logic where the postcondition signals the end of the loop. If the postcondition is true, the loop should exit; if it's true *during* the loop, the reverse execution wouldn't know when to stop.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Floating Point Reversibility
comments.quote: For integer and fixed point number system, $y += f(args...)$ and $y -= f(args...)$ are rigorously reversible... While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors.
comments.explanation: Automatic Differentiation requires high precision for gradients. If the 'reverse computing' relies on $y -= f(x)$ to recover the original $y$, and the authors admit round-off errors exist, these errors could accumulate significantly in long-running loops (e.g., the Fibonacci example in Listing 11 or GMM). The authors claim this is 'safe in most cases' in Appendix F (not provided in full), but the main text lacks a justification of why loss of significance doesn't invalidate the gradient. In AD, even small errors in the backward pass can lead to divergent optimization. This is a technical concern because the core premise is 'Differentiate Everything', but floating-point non-reversibility is a fundamental limit.

comments.title: Numerical/Parameter Inconsistency in GMM Peak Memory
comments.quote: $M = P + 2kd + 2d$ where $d$ and $k$ are the size and number of covariance matrices. $P = d(d+1)k/2 + k + dk$ ... The memory of the dataset ($d \times N$) is not included because it will scale as $N$.
comments.explanation: The authors state that for GMM, NiLang uses 'extra memory... negligible comparing to the original program'. However, the formula provided $M = P + 2kd + 2d$ seems to describe only the parameter/model state. In AD, the overhead typically scales with the number of operations or intermediate variables. Because GMM involves a summation over $N$ data points, a standard AD would store $O(N)$ intermediates. The authors imply that by using reverse computing, they avoid this. However, if 'reverse computing' is used, they must either store the intermediate states or recompute them. If they recompute (Bennett's), the time increases. Table 1 and Fig 4 suggest memory is near-constant relative to $N$, but the provided formula for $M$ does not include any dependency on $N$, which is unusual for a peak memory claim in an AD context unless the entire loop over $N$ is being recomputed for every parameter update, which would be $O(N^2)$ time.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and ambitious approach to automatic differentiation through reversible computing and an embedded DSL (NiLang) in Julia. The core ideas—avoiding stack-based checkpointing in favor of reverse computing, enabling GPU-compatible AD, and achieving memory efficiency—are well-motivated and supported by benchmarks. The design integrates reversible semantics, memory management, and control flow coherently. However, several technical issues in complexity analysis, formula consistency, and justification weaken the rigor. While many claims are substantiated, some derivations are underspecified or contain subtle errors, particularly in the treatment of Bennett’s algorithm and Table 1. These issues are mostly moderate in severity and fixable, but they affect confidence in the comparative claims about time-space trade-offs.

comments.title: Incorrect space complexity in reverse computing claim
comments.quote: The minimum space complexity in reverse computing is _O_ ( _S_ log( _T_ / _S_ )) (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990; Perumalla, 2013), with time complexity O( _T_[1][.][585] ).
comments.explanation: The claim that reverse computing achieves space complexity O(S log(T/S)) with time O(T^1.585) appears to misstate Bennett’s classic result. Bennett's 1989 paper establishes that for a reversible simulation of a T-step irreversible computation using S workspace, the reversible counterpart can run in time O(T^{1+ε}) using space O(S log T), or alternatively in time O(T^2) using O(S) space—this is the standard space-time trade-off. The expression O(S log(T/S)) is not standard and seems to mix asymptotic variables incorrectly. The exponent 1.585 is suggestive of log_2(3) ≈ 1.585, which arises in Bennett’s recursive pebbling strategy when the recursion depth is logarithmic, but the stated space bound does not align with that derivation. The formula should likely be O(S log T) or O(S log(S max / S)) depending on the partitioning, but O(S log(T/S)) is dimensionally inconsistent if T and S are both growing. This misstatement weakens the quantitative comparison with checkpointing in Table 1.

comments.title: Inconsistent time complexity in Table 1 for reverse computing
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the time complexity for the space-efficient reverse computing case is written as O(T (T/S)^0.585), which simplifies to O(T^{1.585}/S^{0.585}). This conflicts with the standard understanding of Bennett’s algorithm, which achieves O(T^2) time with O(S log T) space in its basic form, or O(T^{1+ε}) time for any ε > 0 when optimized. The expression (T/S)^0.585 is not standard and appears to result from a misinterpretation of the recursion depth or branching factor. The 0.585 exponent may stem from log_2(ϕ) where ϕ is the golden ratio, but that does not align with established results. The correct time complexity for the most space-efficient version should be closer to O(T^2), which is consistent with Bennett's original paper. The stated form misrepresents the algorithm's scalability and exaggerates efficiency.

comments.title: Mismatch between figure and formula for η(τ, δ)
comments.quote:
η(t-1, 2) η(t-1, 1)
Q1 Q1
P2 P2 P2
η(t, 3) η(t, 2) η(t, 1)
Q2 Q2
τ + δ Figure 1: (a) Treeverse algorithm for optimal checkpointing. (Griewank, 1992) η(τ, δ) ≡ = δ   
> (ττ+!δδ!)! is the binomial function.
comments.explanation: The definition of η(τ, δ) is presented as δ times some binomial coefficient involving τ and δ, but the formula is garbled due to OCR or formatting issues: ‘(ττ+!δδ!)!’ is not meaningful. The correct expression from Griewank’s treeverse algorithm is η(t, d) = \binom{t+d}{d}, the binomial coefficient, which counts the number of paths in a binomial partitioning. The surrounding text mentions ‘binomially’ partitioning, which aligns with this. However, the formula as written is incomprehensible and cannot be verified. Assuming the intent is η(τ, δ) = \binom{τ+δ}{δ}, the exponents in the figure labels like η(t,1), η(t,2) make sense as scaling factors. But without the correct formula, the reader cannot confirm the complexity derivation that follows. This is likely an OCR artifact, but as presented, it constitutes a major gap in the theoretical foundation.

comments.title: Underspecified implementation of ‘←’ and ‘→’ in memory management
comments.quote: Denote the allocation of a pre-emptied memory as `x` ← `0` , and its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0` .
comments.explanation: The paper introduces `←` and `→` as reversible allocation and deallocation, requiring variables to be zeroed before deallocation. However, it does not specify how this is implemented at the language or runtime level—e.g., whether this is a compile-time guarantee, a runtime assertion, or a semantic convention. In Listing 5, `n` ← `zero(T)` is used, but it's unclear if this allocates new memory or reuses a register. For GPU compatibility, precise memory layout and allocation strategy are critical. The claim that this enables GPU kernel compilation depends on these details, yet no discussion is provided on how memory is managed in practice (e.g., stack vs. heap, register allocation). This underspecification makes it difficult to assess the feasibility of the GPU claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about reversible control flow correctness
comments.quote: To reverse this statement, one can exchange `start` and `stop` and inverse the sign of `step` .
comments.explanation: The text claims that reversing a reversible `for` loop involves swapping `start` and `stop` and negating `step`, but this is only correct if the loop body is reversible and the iterator is restored. The paper does not clarify whether the loop variable is mutated in place or must be restored, nor does it define the precondition/postcondition for `for` loops as it does for `if` and `while`. This creates ambiguity: a reader might incorrectly assume any forward loop can be reversed this way, but without asserting the final iterator value matches the initial (as hinted), reversal could fail. The omission of a formal rule or example for `for` loops (unlike `if` and `while`) reduces clarity where it's needed.

comments.title: Overstated performance advantage in GMM benchmark
comments.quote: NiLang’s objective function is ∼2× slower than normal code due to the uncomputing overhead. In this case, NiLang does not show advantage to Tapenade in obtaining gradients, the ratio between computing the gradients and the objective function are close.
comments.explanation: The paper claims state-of-the-art performance in the abstract and introduction, but the GMM benchmark shows NiLang is slower than baseline code and comparable to Tapenade in gradient computation. While this is honestly reported, the abstract's claim of 'state-of-the-art performance' is not supported by the data presented. The bundle adjustment result on GPU shows speedup, but the GMM result contradicts the general claim. The issue is one of framing: the performance advantage is context-dependent (e.g., GPU, parallelizable problems), but the high-level claim overgeneralizes. Context resolves that it's fast in some cases, but the abstract should reflect this nuance.

comments.title: Missing justification for gradient computation via reverse program
comments.quote: Computing the gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation that computes[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]][.]
comments.explanation: The paper states that computing gradients in the backward pass is 'similar to forward mode', but does not explain how the composition of reversed instructions yields correct adjoints. In standard reverse-mode AD, gradients are accumulated backward; here, the claim is that operator overloading on `GVar` during reversal achieves this, but no derivation or example is provided. The equivalence between reversing a program and correct backpropagation is non-trivial and requires justification—e.g., chain rule preservation under reversal. Appendix C is referenced, but within the main text, this is a skipped critical step. A reader cannot verify the correctness without assuming the implementation works.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to automatic differentiation using reversible programming, introducing the NiLang eDSL. The core idea of using reverse computing instead of checkpointing to reduce memory overhead is novel and potentially impactful. The implementation details and benchmarks demonstrate promising results, particularly for scientific computing applications. However, the paper suffers from several mathematical notation issues, missing details in complexity analysis, and some unclear claims that need clarification. The writing could be improved with more precise mathematical formulations and clearer explanations of key algorithms.

comments.title: Unclear time complexity notation for reverse computing
comments.explanation: The time complexity for the most space-efficient reverse computing case is written as O(T(T/S)^0.585), which is mathematically ambiguous. It's unclear whether this means O(T^(1+0.585) / S^0.585) or O(T × (T/S)^0.585). The notation should be clarified to explicitly show the dependency on both T and S. This appears to be referring to Bennett's algorithm but the exact formulation is not standard or clearly explained.

comments.title: Missing explicit formula for Bennett's algorithm overhead
comments.quote: Here, the overhead in time is polynomial, which is worse than the treeverse algorithm.
comments.explanation: The paper claims Bennett's algorithm has polynomial time overhead but doesn't provide the explicit formula. This makes it difficult to verify the claim that it's worse than treeverse. The reference to Bennett's work doesn't substitute for stating the actual complexity analysis here. A clear formula should be provided to match the level of detail given for checkpointing.

comments.title: Garbled mathematical notation for adjoint composition
comments.explanation: The equation attempting to show that applying adjoint twice returns to identity is severely garbled with formatting issues. It appears to be trying to express (∂y/∂x) * (∂x/∂y) = 1, but the notation is illegible. This is a fundamental property being claimed and deserves clear mathematical notation.

comments.title: Unclear memory usage formula for GMM
comments.quote: _P_ = _[d]_[(] _[d]_[+][1][)] _k_ + _k_ + _dk_ is the size of 2 parameter space.
comments.explanation: The formula for P has formatting issues that make it ambiguous. It appears to be attempting to express P = d(d+1)k/2 + k + dk, but the notation is unclear with misplaced brackets and operators. This formula is important for understanding the memory analysis in Figure 4.

comments.title: Insufficient justification of floating-point reversibility
comments.quote: However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion.
comments.explanation: While the paper acknowledges that floating-point operations aren't strictly reversible, it proceeds to treat them as reversible throughout. The brief mention that this is "safe in most cases provided careful implementation" (referencing Appendix F) is insufficient justification. The implications of this approximation on gradient correctness should be more thoroughly discussed in the main text.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting framework, NiLang, which leverages the concept of 'reverse computing' to implement a reversible eDSL for automatic differentiation (AD). The authors argue convincingly that their approach avoids the overhead associated with implicit stack management and checkpointing common in traditional AD engines, particularly for scientific computing and GPU-resident tasks. However, the manuscript suffers from significant inconsistencies in mathematical notation and claims related to algorithmic complexity, which detract from the formal rigor of the work. Specifically, the presentation of the time-space trade-off results is mathematically ambiguous due to symbols that are not defined within the text or table, and some of the cited complexity results contain typographical errors that misrepresent the well-known bounds.

comments.title: Unexplained variables in complexity table
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the authors provide time and space complexity expressions for 'Reverse computing'. The term involving '0.585' is a known result for the pebble game algorithm (Bennett, 1989), but the expression formatted as 'O(T( T / S )^0.585)' is notationally messy. Furthermore, the variable 'S' is inconsistent: in the text leading up to the table, 'S' refers to both the set of checkpointed states and the run-time memory capacity. It is unclear if S here represents the number of checkpoints or the memory budget. This ambiguity makes it difficult for a reader to verify the scaling behavior.

comments.title: Dimensionally inconsistent formula in binomial definition
comments.explanation: The formula provided in Figure 1 for the binomial function is syntactically broken as 'δ (τ+δ)! | (τ!δ!)'. Assuming a vertical bar '|' is a typo for a division operator or fraction, the expression would be δ * combinations(τ+δ, δ). However, the standard treeverse complexity for the number of steps is usually given by the binomial coefficient (τ+δ)! / (τ!δ!), not multiplied by δ as stated. If the authors intend to define the total number of operations, the extra factor of δ is non-standard and lacks justification.

comments.title: Under-specified reverse compilation strategy
comments.quote: a regular program can be transpiled to the reversible style is by doing the transformation in Listing. 1.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that a regular program can be converted to reversible style using the transformation in Listing 1 (which shows augmenting updates like '+='). This claims that any irreversible program can be made reversible by simply changing operators to their additive/XOR counterparts. This ignores the Landauer limit mentioned elsewhere in the paper; simply changing operators does not ensure the program is bijective unless the mapping is invertible (e.g., discarding bits is not reversible). This oversimplifies the fundamental challenge of reversible translation.

comments.title: OCR or Transposition error in Bennett's recurrence
comments.explanation: In Section 6, the authors discuss energy efficiency and mention an exponential relation for uncomputing memory, written as 'Qn = (2 k − 1) [n]'. This notation is mathematically incoherent and likely contains an OCR error or a significant typo in the exponent variable. Given the context of the Section 2 Bennett algorithm discussion, the formula likely represents a recurrence relation or a power-law complexity; as written, it is impossible for a reader to interpret the relationship between Q, k, and n.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel approach to reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) by implementing a reversible embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) called NiLang in Julia. The core idea is to replace traditional checkpointing with reverse computing to trace intermediate states, avoiding implicit stack operations and enabling better compatibility with compiler optimizations and GPU kernels. The paper is ambitious and addresses real performance bottlenecks in general-purpose AD. However, it contains several significant issues: a critical mathematical error in the complexity analysis of the Bennett algorithm (incorrect exponent), a lack of rigorous justification for treating floating-point operations as reversible, unclear definitions and notation regarding memory overhead (particularly the 'S' variable), and a questionable claim about the practical superiority of reverse computing over checkpointing based on flawed complexity formulas and informal arguments. The benchmarks show competitive performance in some tasks, but the theoretical foundations need substantial clarification and correction. Overall, the contribution is interesting but the presentation is marred by technical inaccuracies.

comments.title: Incorrect complexity exponent for Bennett's algorithm
comments.quote: The minimum space complexity in reverse computing is O(S log(T/S)) (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990; Perumalla, 2013), with time complexity O(T^(1.585)).
comments.explanation: The quoted time complexity O(T^{1.585}) does not match the classic result for Bennett's algorithm. Bennett (1973, 1989) shows a time-space trade-off where for a computation of T steps and space S (the space of the original irreversible program), the reversible simulation can use space O(S log(T/S)) and time O(T^{1 + log(k)} / log(k)) for some parameter k, or more familiarly, a family of algorithms where time T^(1+ε) for a space overhead factor. The exponent 1.585 is log_2(3) ≈ 1.585, which would arise from dividing the computation into 3 sectors at each recursive level. However, this is not the exponent for the 'minimum space' scenario — that would typically be O(T^{2}) in checkpointing's case or O(T^{1+ε}) in Bennett's for ε > 0. The context suggests the authors intended to express the time complexity for a specific trade-off point, but they did not define the parameter 'k' or how the exponent arises. The statement as written is ambiguous and potentially misleading, as it gives a single numerical exponent without specifying the space usage associated with it. The complexity formulas in Table 1 are also incorrect: the time complexity for the most space-efficient reverse computing is not O(T(T/S)^0.585). The exponent should depend on the base of the partition (e.g., if each recursion splits into k parts, time is O(T^{log_k(2k-1)})), and the space is O(S log_k(T/S)). The specific exponent 0.585 suggests k=3 (log_3(5) ≈ 1.465? no, 2*3-1=5, log_3(5)=1.465; 0.585 is log_2(3)-1? unclear). This is a major mathematical error in the core complexity comparison.

comments.title: Floating-point reversibility claim is not justified
comments.quote: For convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that floating-point addition and subtraction can be treated as reversible by ignoring rounding errors. This is a bold claim that contradicts standard numerical analysis. Reversibility requires that applying the inverse operation (subtraction after addition, or vice versa) recovers the exact original state. For any finite-precision floating-point system, (a + b) - b != a in general due to rounding. The paper references Appendix F for justification, but in the provided text, Appendix F is not included. Unless Appendix F provides a rigorous proof or a careful error analysis showing that the gradient computation is still accurate despite this non-reversibility (e.g., via a correct implementation that stores lost bits or uses a compensated algorithm), the reader cannot accept the claim. This is a serious gap because the entire AD framework relies on exact reversal to trace intermediate states. If the primitive operations are not truly reversible, the correctness of the gradient computation is thrown into doubt. The authors need to either provide the justification or explicitly handle the errors.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'S' in complexity analysis
comments.quote: In the checkpointing scheme, the program first takes snapshots of states at certain time steps S = {s_a, s_b, ...}, 1 ≤ a < b < ... ≤ T by running a forward pass.
comments.explanation: The symbol 'S' is used inconsistently. In the introduction (Sec. 2, line 1), 'S' is defined as the set of snapshot states. Later in Table 1, 'S' is used as a scalar representing the 'space of the original irreversible program'. This is a crucial ambiguity. In the checkpointing and reverse computing literature, the 'S' in the complexity formulas O(T) and O(T^2) typically denotes the space per state (or the size of a single state), not the total memory used. The authors use 'S' in the table as if it's a scalar variable representing the space per step, which is fine, but then in the text, they define 'S' as a set of states. This inconsistency could confuse a careful reader. The text should clearly distinguish between the set of snapshot states and the space complexity measure. The statement 'with a run-time memory S' earlier in Sec. 2 is also vague — does this mean the total memory allocated, or the memory per time step? This ambiguity propagates to Table 1, where the units of 'T' and 'S' are unclear.

comments.title: Misleading comparison of time-space trade-off: 'only checkpointing can achieve zero space overhead'
comments.quote: On the other side, only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead by recomputing everything from the beginning s_0, with a time complexity O(T^2). The minimum space complexity in reverse computing is O(S log(T/S))
comments.explanation: This comparison is misleading. The statement that 'only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead' is technically correct for the specific method described (recompute from beginning), but it ignores that reverse computing can also achieve arbitrarily low space by using Bennett's recursive algorithm, albeit at a superlinear time cost. The quoted space complexity O(S log(T/S)) for reverse computing is not the 'minimum' space achievable— it is the space for a specific trade-off (the 'pebble game' minimal space). Actually, Bennett's algorithm can use O(S) space with time O(T^2) as well, by setting the recursion factor appropriately. The authors seem to imply that reverse computing has an inherent lower bound of O(log T) overhead, which is true only if you demand a specific time complexity. But checkpointing can also achieve O(log T) space overhead with the treeverse algorithm. The key difference is not 'zero vs log' but rather the precise trade-off curves. The authors should present the families of trade-offs for both methods on equal footing, not cherry-pick the extreme points. Furthermore, the claim 'only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead' is contradicted later in Appendix B.3 where a constant-memory implementation is shown — showing reverse computing can also use space independent of T for certain algorithms (like chained multiplication).

comments.title: Contradictory statements about memory allocation in reverse computing
comments.quote: reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users, user can optimize the memory access patterns for their own devices like GPU.
comments.explanation: This statement is contradictory. Throughout the paper, reverse computing is described as requiring explicit allocation (e.g., x ← 0 for ancilla). Saying it 'does not allocate automatically' is a design choice of NiLang (forcing the user to manage memory), not a property of reverse computing in general. In fact, reverse computing algorithms like Bennett's do allocate intermediate storage (the 'copy' buffers). The phrasing could mislead readers into thinking reverse computing automatically eliminates memory allocation overhead. The authors should clarify that NiLang explicitly requires the user to manage memory, which is a feature (transparency) but also a burden. The paper elsewhere claims this is an advantage, but it is at odds with the 'ease of use' argument. This is a minor inconsistency but could confuse.

comments.title: Underspecified Bennett algorithm pseudocode: missing function signatures and variable scoping
comments.quote: bennett({s_1, ...}, k, i, len) if len == 1 s_{i+1} ← 0 f_i(s_{i+1}, s_i) else # P process that calls the forward program k steps bennett({s_1, ...}, k, i+len ÷ k*(j-1), len ÷ k) for j=1,2,...,k # Q process that calls the backward program k-1 steps ~bennett({s_1, ...}, k, i+len ÷ k*(j-1), len ÷ k) for j=k-1,k-2,...,1
comments.explanation: The pseudocode for Bennett's algorithm is too vague to be reproducible. Key details are missing: (1) The state collection '{s_1,...}' is not clearly typed or structured— what does it mean to pass a collection of states by value/reference? (2) The notation 's_{i+1} ← 0' suggests global state allocation, but it's unclear how the index i relates to the state array. (3) The division `len ÷ k` assumes integer division, and it's not stated what happens if len is not divisible by k. (4) The algorithm recursively calls itself for the P and Q processes, but the termination condition `len == 1` only handles the base case of a single step; it doesn't show how the adjacency (the copy step) is handled for larger segments. (5) The inverse function `~bennett` is said to exist but its definition is not provided — is it automatically generated? (6) The algorithm assumes that the function `f_i` can be reversed, but the reversible version (with += allocation) is not shown. (7) The 'compute-copy-uncompute' pattern is mentioned but not explicitly encoded in the pseudocode; the copy step (output duplication) is missing. As a result, a reader cannot implement this algorithm from the description. The paper claims a NiLang implementation is in Appendix A, but that appendix is not provided. This is a critical gap for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: 'P' and 'Q' processes used in figure but not defined
comments.quote: Figure 1: (b) Bennett’s time space trade-off scheme for reverse computing. (Bennett, 1973; Levine & Sherman, 1990) P and Q are computing and uncomputing respectively.
comments.explanation: In the caption of Figure 1(b), P and Q are introduced as 'computing and uncomputing' respectively. However, in the pseudocode in Listing 3, the terms 'P process' and 'Q process' are used in comments but not formally defined. Furthermore, in the figure labels, 'P1', 'P2', 'Q1', 'Q2' appear, but it's unclear what the subscripts denote (recursion depth? sector index?). The text says 'The pseudo-code is defined in Listing. 3', but Listing 3 uses `bennett` for both P and Q. This leaves ambiguity: is Q just the inverse of P? The figure suggests Q is a backward sweep, but the pseudocode uses `~bennett` (inverse of bennett) for the Q process. That is consistent if `bennett` includes both forward and backward sweeps for sub-sections. But then the caption says 'P and Q are computing and uncomputing' which could mislead readers into thinking P is purely forward and Q purely backward, when in reality each recursive call of `bennett` contains both P and Q. These details should be clarified in the text.

comments.title: Overclaim: 'reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users' as an advantage without evidence
comments.quote: Second, reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users, user can optimize the memory access patterns for their own devices like GPU. Third, reverse computing is compatible with effective codes, so that it fits better with modern languages.
comments.explanation: The claim that automatic allocation is harmful and that manual memory management by the user is an advantage is presented as a fact without evidence. While there are real issues with automatic checkpointing in traditional AD (e.g., excessive memory), it does not follow that manual management is inherently better. The paper does not provide a quantitative comparison showing that manual management in NiLang yields better performance than automatic optimization in other systems for comparable tasks. The example given (bundle adjustment GPU speedup) is impressive, but the 200× speedup could be due to many factors (e.g., using a GPU, better algorithm, etc.), not solely the lack of automatic allocation. The authors should provide a controlled comparison (e.g., same algorithm, same hardware, with and without automatic allocation) to substantiate this claim. Without such evidence, this reads as an opinion rather than a proven advantage.

comments.title: Missing justification for gradient computing via operator overloading on reversed program
comments.quote: Inspired by the Julia package ForwardDiff (Revels et al., 2016), we use the operator overloading technique to differentiate the program efficiently. In the backward pass, we wrap each output variable with a composite type GVar that containing an extra gradient field, and feed it into the reversed generic program.
comments.explanation: This description of the AD implementation is too high-level to be reproducible. The key step is: 'Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of GVar at the meantime of uncomputing.' How exactly are the gradient rules defined for each reversible instruction? The paper says that the adjoint of a function can be defined on either the function or its reverse, and that applying both gives identity. But it does not explain how to compute the gradient for a complex instruction like a loop or recursion. For example, how is the gradient of a reversible while loop computed? The paper mentions that the reverse program is fed with GVar variables, but GVar needs to carry both the primal and the gradient. The interplay between uncomputing (erasing states) and updating gradients is not explained. A reader would not know how to implement the AD system from this description. The claim that it's 'similar to forward mode' is misleading because reverse mode requires storing intermediate gradients (adjoints), which forward mode does not. The details in Appendix C are missing. This is a major gap for a paper that introduces a new AD method.

comments.title: Misstatement: 'ForwardDiff memory usage is approximately the original size times the batch size'
comments.quote: The ForwardDiff memory usage is approximately the original size times the batch size, where the batch size is 12 by default.
comments.explanation: This statement about ForwardDiff's memory usage appears in the context of GMM benchmarking. ForwardDiff uses forward-mode AD with dual numbers; the 'batch size' refers to the chunk size for Jacobian computation, not a neural network batch. For a single-output objective (as in GMM), forward-mode AD with a batch size of 12 would compute 12 partial derivatives at once using 12 dual numbers per variable. The memory usage is indeed O(original memory * batch_size), but the phrase 'original size times the batch size' could be misinterpreted by readers unfamiliar with forward-mode chunking. Also, the sentence 'the batch size is 12 by default' should reference the ForwardDiff package default (which is indeed typically chosen based on word size). This is a minor clarification, but it's important for reproducibility of the benchmark. Additionally, the paper says 'ForwardDiff data is missing due to not finishing in limited time', which is vague — this is a benchmark omission, not an error, but it should be noted as a limitation.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting reversible eDSL for automatic differentiation, demonstrating strong performance in benchmarks and addressing a critical space overhead issue in standard AD tools. However, the theoretical foundation in Section 2 is significantly hampered by severe OCR artifacts in the mathematical notation, rendering the definitions of key complexities unintelligible. Furthermore, Section 5 contains a mathematically questionable formula for the memory usage in the GMM benchmarks where the parameter count for covariance matrices is doubled without clear justification for the term '2 parameter space'. These issues undermine the rigor of the theoretical comparison and the reproducibility of the analytical cost estimates.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes NiLang, a reversible embedded DSL in Julia designed to replace traditional checkpointing with reverse computing for memory-efficient reverse-mode automatic differentiation. The approach tackles a genuine bottleneck in scientific computing, and the language design demonstrates thoughtful handling of reversible control flow and explicit memory management. However, the manuscript contains critical gaps: key mathematical formulas for time-space complexity and benchmark memory analysis are missing from the text, hindering reproducibility. More significantly, a foundational claim in Section 4.1 regarding the equivalence of adjoints and program reversals appears mathematically problematic for general differentiable operations, as it conflates Jacobian transposition with inversion. Additionally, ambiguities in benchmark descriptions and notation inconsistencies in Table 1 further weaken the technical rigor. With clarified theoretical foundations and restored equations, the work could make a meaningful contribution to the reversible AD community.
